title: A long straight wire carries a current of $5\,\text{A}$. The magnetic field at a distance of $10\,\text{cm}$ from the wire is ________ $\times 10^{-6}\,\text{T}$.
solution_text: The magnetic field due to a long straight current-carrying wire at distance $d$ is $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi d}$. Given: $I = 5\,\text{A}$, $d = 10\,\text{cm} = 0.1\,\text{m}$, and $\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7} \implies \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$. Substituting: $B = \frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{0.1} = \frac{10^{-6}}{0.1} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{T}$. Hence, the coefficient of $10^{-6}$ is 10.
common_mistake: Using the formula for the center of a circular loop ($B = \mu_0 I / 2R$) or incorrect unit conversion of distance (using 10 instead of 0.1 m).

title: A circular arc of radius $R$ subtends an angle of $60^\circ$ at its center. If it carries a current $I$, the magnetic field at the center of the arc is:
notes: Magnetic field at the center of a circular loop is $B_0 = \mu_0 I / (2R)$. An arc subtending angle $\theta$ produces $B = B_0 \left(\frac{\theta}{2\pi}\right)$. Here $\theta = 60^\circ = \pi/3$.
solution_text: The magnetic field at the center of a circular loop of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is $B_{\text{loop}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$. An arc of angle $\theta$ (in radians) produces a field proportional to its fraction of a full circle: $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} \left(\frac{\theta}{2\pi}\right) = \frac{\mu_0 I \theta}{4\pi R}$. Given $\theta = 60^\circ = \frac{\pi}{3}\,\text{rad}$: $B = \frac{\mu_0 I (\pi/3)}{4\pi R} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{12R}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Using $\theta = 60$ directly in the formula without converting to radians, or using the straight wire formula.

title: A proton and an $\alpha$-particle (helium nucleus) enter a uniform perpendicular magnetic field with the same kinetic energy. The ratio of the radius of the proton's circular path to that of the $\alpha$-particle's path ($r_p / r_\alpha$) is:
notes: Radius in magnetic field: $r = \frac{mv}{qB} = \frac{\sqrt{2mK}}{qB}$ where $K$ is kinetic energy. Ratio $r_p / r_\alpha = \frac{\sqrt{m_p}/q_p}{\sqrt{m_\alpha}/q_\alpha}$. Note: $m_\alpha = 4m_p$, $q_\alpha = 2q_p$.
solution_text: The radius of a charged particle's circular path in a perpendicular magnetic field is given by $r = \frac{mv}{qB} = \frac{\sqrt{2mK}}{qB}$, where $K$ is the kinetic energy. At constant $K$ and $B$, $r \propto \frac{\sqrt{m}}{q}$. Let $m_p = m$ and $q_p = q$. For the $\alpha$-particle, $m_\alpha = 4m$ and $q_\alpha = 2q$. The ratio is: $\frac{r_p}{r_\alpha} = \frac{\sqrt{m_p}/q_p}{\sqrt{m_\alpha}/q_\alpha} = \frac{\sqrt{m}/q}{\sqrt{4m}/(2q)} = \frac{\sqrt{m}/q}{2\sqrt{m}/(2q)} = 1$. Thus, the ratio is $1:1$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using $r \propto m/q$ (which only holds for constant velocity, yielding $1:2$) or using constant momentum relation.

title: Two long parallel wires, separated by a distance of $10\,\text{cm}$, carry currents of $2\,\text{A}$ and $5\,\text{A}$ in the same direction. The magnitude of the force per unit length between the wires is ________ $\times 10^{-6}\,\text{N/m}$.
solution_text: The force per unit length $f = F/L$ between two parallel wires separated by distance $d$ carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ is $f = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$. Given: $I_1 = 2\,\text{A}$, $I_2 = 5\,\text{A}$, $d = 10\,\text{cm} = 0.1\,\text{m}$. Substituting: $f = \frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 5}{0.1} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1} = 200 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{N/m} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{N/m}$. Hence, the coefficient of $10^{-6}$ is 20.
common_mistake: Confusing the direction of forces (same direction attracts, opposite repels) or wrong numerical calculations of the constants.

title: A long straight solid cylindrical conductor of radius $R$ carries a steady current $I$ uniformly distributed across its cross-section. The magnetic field $B$ at a distance $r$ from the axis is:

options.A: proportional to $r$ for $r < R$, and proportional to $1/r$ for $r > R$
options.B: proportional to $1/r$ for both $r < R$ and $r > R$
options.C: zero for $r < R$, and proportional to $1/r$ for $r > R$
options.D: proportional to $r^2$ for $r < R$, and proportional to $1/r$ for $r > R$

notes: Use Ampere's Law: $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{encl}}$. For $r < R$, $I_{\text{encl}} = I(r^2/R^2)$. For $r > R$, $I_{\text{encl}} = I$.
solution_text: 1) Inside the cylinder ($r < R$): consider an Amperean loop of radius $r$. $\oint B dl = B(2\pi r) = \mu_0 I_{\text{encl}} = \mu_0 I \left(\frac{\pi r^2}{\pi R^2}\right) \implies B = \frac{\mu_0 I r}{2\pi R^2} \propto r$. 2) Outside the cylinder ($r > R$): $I_{\text{encl}} = I$. $B(2\pi r) = \mu_0 I \implies B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} \propto \frac{1}{r}$. Therefore, $B \propto r$ inside, and $B \propto 1/r$ outside. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking the magnetic field inside a solid conductor is zero (which is only true for electric fields in electrostatics, or for hollow cylinders).

title: A square loop of side $10\,\text{cm}$ consists of $20$ turns and carries a current of $2\,\text{A}$. The loop is suspended vertically in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of $0.5\,\text{T}$. If the plane of the loop makes an angle of $30^\circ$ with the direction of the magnetic field, the torque acting on the loop is: (Note: angle $\theta$ in torque formula $\tau = N I A B \sin\theta$ is between the normal to the loop and the field)
notes: Torque is $\tau = N I A B \sin\alpha$ where $\alpha$ is the angle between the normal to the loop plane and the field. If loop plane makes $30^\circ$ with field, then $\alpha = 90^\circ - 30^\circ = 60^\circ$. Area $A = (0.1)^2 = 0.01\,\text{m}^2$.
solution_text: The torque acting on a loop of $N$ turns carrying current $I$ in field $B$ is $\tau = N I A B \sin\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the angle of the normal of the loop with the magnetic field. The plane of the loop makes $30^\circ$ with the field, so $\alpha = 90^\circ - 30^\circ = 60^\circ$. Area $A = L^2 = (0.1\,\text{m})^2 = 0.01\,\text{m}^2$. Substituting: $\tau = 20 \times 2 \times 0.01 \times 0.5 \times \sin 60^\circ = 0.2 \times 0.866 = 0.1732\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m} \approx 0.17\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$. Option D is correct.
common_mistake: Using the given angle $30^\circ$ directly as $\alpha$ in the torque formula, yielding $0.10\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$.

title: A circular loop of radius $R = 10\,\text{cm}$ is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field varying with time as $B = B_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ where $B_0 = 0.5\,\text{T}$ and $\tau = 2\,\text{s}$. The magnitude of the induced EMF in the loop at $t = 2\,\text{s}$ is ________ $\pi \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$. (Take $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$)
solution_text: Magnetic flux is $\Phi = B \cdot A = B_0 e^{-t/\tau} \cdot \pi R^2$. Induced EMF magnitude is $\epsilon = \left|\frac{d\Phi}{dt}\right| = \frac{B_0 \pi R^2}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$. Given: $R = 0.1\,\text{m} \implies R^2 = 0.01$, $B_0 = 0.5\,\text{T}$, $\tau = 2\,\text{s}$. At $t = 2\,\text{s}$: $\epsilon = \frac{0.5 \times \pi \times 0.01}{2} e^{-2/2} = 0.0025 \pi e^{-1} = 2.5 \pi \times 10^{-3} e^{-1} \approx 0.92 \pi \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$... wait, if the question asks for the coefficient of $\pi \times 10^{-3} e^{-1}\,\text{V}$ it would be 2.5. Let's look at the wording: if we state the answer in terms of $\pi e^{-1} \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$ it is 2.5, or if we multiply by $e^{-1} \approx 0.37$, we get $0.92 \pi \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$. Let's adjust the question format so it asks for the value of EMF at $t=0$, which makes it independent of $e$: at $t=0$, $\epsilon = 2.5 \pi \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$. The coefficient is 2.5. Let's make this change to prevent rounding confusion.
title: A circular loop of radius $R = 10\,\text{cm}$ is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field varying with time as $B = B_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ where $B_0 = 0.5\,\text{T}$ and $\tau = 2\,\text{s}$. The magnitude of the induced EMF in the loop at the initial instant $t = 0$ is ________ $\pi \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$.
solution_text: The magnetic flux through the circular loop is $\Phi = B A = \left(B_0 e^{-t/\tau}\right) \pi R^2$. By Faraday's Law, the magnitude of induced EMF is $\epsilon = \left|\frac{d\Phi}{dt}\right| = \frac{B_0 \pi R^2}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$. Given: $R = 10\,\text{cm} = 0.1\,\text{m} \implies R^2 = 0.01\,\text{m}^2$, $B_0 = 0.5\,\text{T}$, $\tau = 2\,\text{s}$. At $t = 0$: $\epsilon = \frac{0.5 \times \pi \times 0.01}{2} e^0 = 0.0025 \pi\,\text{V} = 2.5\pi \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$. Thus, the coefficient is 2.5.
common_mistake: Forgetting to differentiate the exponential term, or not converting the radius to meters.

title: A conducting rod of length $L = 1\,\text{m}$ is rotated with constant angular velocity $\omega = 10\,\text{rad/s}$ about one of its ends in a perpendicular uniform magnetic field $B = 0.4\,\text{T}$. The potential difference induced between the two ends of the rod is ________ $\text{V}$.
solution_text: The induced EMF in a rotating rod of length $L$ about one end in a perpendicular magnetic field is $\epsilon = \frac{1}{2} B \omega L^2$. Given: $B = 0.4\,\text{T}$, $\omega = 10\,\text{rad/s}$, $L = 1\,\text{m}$. Substituting: $\epsilon = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.4 \times 10 \times (1)^2 = 2\,\text{V}$.
common_mistake: Using the linear motional EMF formula $\epsilon = B v L$ with end velocity, forgetting the factor of $1/2$ that comes from averaging velocity along the rod.

title: A cylindrical bar magnet is released from rest along the axis of a long, vertical, copper tube. As the magnet falls through the tube, its acceleration will:

options.B: continually decrease until the magnet comes to rest
options.C: continually decrease until it becomes zero, after which the magnet falls with constant velocity
options.D: continually increase as it falls faster

notes: By Lenz's Law, the motion of the magnet induces eddy currents in the copper tube which produce a magnetic force opposing the fall. This drag force is proportional to velocity: $F_d = -kv$.
solution_text: When the magnet falls, the changing magnetic flux induces eddy currents in the copper tube. By Lenz's Law, these currents produce a magnetic field that opposes the motion of the magnet, exerting an upward magnetic force $F_m$ on it. The drag force is proportional to the velocity of the magnet: $F_m = kv$. The equation of motion is $mg - kv = ma \implies a = g - \frac{k}{m}v$. As velocity $v$ increases, the upward drag force increases, so the acceleration $a$ decreases. When $v$ reaches terminal velocity $v_t = \frac{mg}{k}$, the acceleration becomes zero, and the magnet falls with a constant terminal speed. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking the copper tube will attract the magnet and accelerate it faster than $g$, or that the magnet will stop completely.

title: Two coaxial solenoids are made such that one is inside the other. The inner solenoid has radius $r_1$, length $L$, and $N_1$ turns. The outer solenoid has radius $r_2$, length $L$, and $N_2$ turns. If $r_1 \ll r_2$, the mutual inductance $M$ of the solenoids is given by:
notes: Pass current $I$ through outer solenoid: $B_2 = \mu_0 N_2 I / L$. The flux through inner solenoid is $\Phi_{12} = N_1 (B_2 A_1) = N_1 \left(\frac{\mu_0 N_2 I}{L}\right) \pi r_1^2$. Mutual inductance is $\Phi_{12} / I$.
solution_text: Let a current $I$ flow through the outer solenoid. The magnetic field inside it is uniform: $B_2 = \frac{\mu_0 N_2 I}{L}$. Since the inner solenoid is inside, the magnetic flux passing through each turn of the inner solenoid is $\phi_1 = B_2 A_1 = \left(\frac{\mu_0 N_2 I}{L}\right) \pi r_1^2$. The total flux linkage of the inner solenoid is $\Phi_1 = N_1 \phi_1 = \frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2 N_1 N_2}{L} I$. The mutual inductance is $M = \frac{\Phi_1}{I} = \frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2 N_1 N_2}{L}$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Using the area of the outer solenoid $\pi r_2^2$ (which is incorrect because the magnetic field of the outer solenoid only links the inner solenoid over its own cross-section $\pi r_1^2$).

title: In a cyclotron, the time period of revolution of a charged particle in the perpendicular magnetic field is independent of its:
notes: Time period of circular path is $T = 2\pi m / (qB)$. It is independent of speed and radius.
solution_text: The time period of revolution of a charged particle in a circular orbit perpendicular to a magnetic field is given by $T = \frac{2\pi r}{v}$. Since the radius of the orbit is $r = \frac{mv}{qB}$, we substitute $r$: $T = \frac{2\pi}{v} \left(\frac{mv}{qB}\right) = \frac{2\pi m}{qB}$. As we can see, $T$ depends on the mass $m$, charge $q$, and magnetic field $B$, but is completely independent of the speed $v$ (and radius $r$) of the particle. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking that because $T = 2\pi r / v$ it must depend on speed, forgetting that radius $r$ increases proportionally with speed $v$.

title: An inductor of self-inductance $L = 5\,\text{H}$ carries a steady current of $2\,\text{A}$. The magnetic energy stored in the inductor is ________ $\text{J}$.
solution_text: The magnetic energy stored in an inductor carrying current $I$ is given by $U = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$. Substituting the given values: $L = 5\,\text{H}$ and $I = 2\,\text{A}$: $U = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times (2)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 4 = 10\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Using $U = L I^2$ or forgetting the factor of $1/2$ (similar to kinetic energy $1/2 mv^2$).

title: A magnetic needle of magnetic dipole moment $M = 2 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{J/T}$ is placed in a uniform magnetic field $B = 0.3\,\text{T}$. The work done in rotating the needle by $90^\circ$ from its stable equilibrium position is ________ $\times 10^{-3}\,\text{J}$.
solution_text: The potential energy of a magnetic dipole in a magnetic field is $U(\theta) = -M B \cos\theta$. 1) Stable equilibrium: $\theta_1 = 0^\circ \implies U_1 = -M B$. 2) After rotation by $90^\circ$: $\theta_2 = 90^\circ \implies U_2 = -M B \cos 90^\circ = 0$. The work done is $W = U_2 - U_1 = 0 - (-M B) = M B$. Substituting: $W = (2 \times 10^{-2}) \times (0.3) = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{J}$. Hence, the coefficient of $10^{-3}$ is 6.
common_mistake: Using $W = M B (1 - \cos\theta)$ but substituting $\theta = 0$ or doing rotation from $180^\circ$.

title: An inductor of self-inductance $L$ and resistance $R$ is connected in series with a battery of EMF $V_0$ at $t=0$. The time constant of this LR circuit is $\tau = L/R$. The current in the circuit reaches $63\%$ of its steady-state maximum value at time $t$ equal to:
notes: The growth of current in an LR circuit is $I(t) = I_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ where $\tau = L/R$. At $t=\tau$, $I = I_0(1 - 1/e) \approx 0.63 I_0$.
solution_text: The growth of current in a series LR circuit is given by $I(t) = I_0\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$, where $I_0 = V_0/R$ is the steady-state maximum current, and $\tau = L/R$ is the inductive time constant. At $t = \tau = L/R$: $I(\tau) = I_0(1 - e^{-1}) = I_0(1 - 0.368) \approx 0.632 I_0 = 63.2\%\,I_0$. Therefore, the current reaches approximately $63\%$ of its maximum value in one time constant, which is $L/R$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using $\tau = RC$ (which is for RC circuits) or inverting the ratio as $R/L$.

title: A square loop of side $a$ is placed at a distance $a$ from a long straight wire carrying a current $I$. The loop and the wire are coplanar. The magnetic flux through the loop is $\frac{\mu_0 I a}{2\pi} \ln x$. The value of $x$ is ________.
solution_text: Consider a thin strip of the loop parallel to the wire, of width $dx$ at distance $x$ from the wire. The magnetic field at this strip is $B(x) = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi x}$. The area of this strip is $dA = a dx$. The magnetic flux through this strip is $d\Phi = B(x) dA = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi x} a dx = \frac{\mu_0 I a}{2\pi} \frac{dx}{x}$. The total flux through the loop is found by integrating from $x=a$ to $x=2a$: $\Phi = \int_a^{2a} \frac{\mu_0 I a}{2\pi} \frac{dx}{x} = \frac{\mu_0 I a}{2\pi} [\ln x]_a^{2a} = \frac{\mu_0 I a}{2\pi} \ln\left(\frac{2a}{a}\right) = \frac{\mu_0 I a}{2\pi} \ln 2$. Comparing this with $\frac{\mu_0 I a}{2\pi} \ln x$, we get $x = 2$.
common_mistake: Integrating over incorrect limits (like $0$ to $a$, which is singular, or $a$ to $3a$), or using a constant field approximation $B = \mu_0 I / 2\pi a$ which is incorrect because the field varies across the loop width.
